parentQuestion: Glands whose secretions are stored and then released when the cell ruptures and dies are classified as ___.

options: An organ that produces and releases substances
scaffolds.explain: A GLAND is an ORGAN that PRODUCES and RELEASES SUBSTANCES. Glands make hormones, sweat, saliva, oil, digestive enzymes, and more.

wrongExplains.2: Nerves transmit signals; glands produce secretions.
wrongExplains.3: Blood vessels transport blood; glands release products.

scaffolds.q: What are the three main modes of glandular secretion?
scaffolds.explain: The three secretion modes are MEROCRINE, APOCRINE, and HOLOCRINE. Each describes HOW the cell releases its product — from gentle exocytosis to complete cell destruction.

wrongExplains.0: These describe transport mechanisms, not secretion modes.
wrongExplains.2: These describe gland types, not secretion mechanisms.

options: The cell releases products by exocytosis and stays intact
Part of the cell pinches off
The cell transforms into a different type
scaffolds.explain: In MEROCRINE secretion, vesicles release contents by EXOCYTOSIS and the CELL STAYS INTACT. The cell survives and continues secreting. Sweat glands work this way.

wrongExplains.0: Cell rupture and death describes holocrine secretion.

options: Part of the cell pinches off with the secretion
The entire cell ruptures and dies
scaffolds.explain: In APOCRINE secretion, the CELL'S APEX BREAKS OFF with the secretion. The cell survives but loses a piece. Some mammary gland secretions work this way.

wrongExplains.2: Complete rupture and death describes holocrine secretion.
wrongExplains.3: Cell division is mitosis, not secretion.

options: The entire cell fills with product, dies, and ruptures to release it
scaffolds.explain: In HOLOCRINE secretion, the ENTIRE CELL fills with product, DIES, and RUPTURES. 'Holo-' means whole/entire — the whole cell becomes the product.

wrongExplains.2: The entire cell contents are released, not just the nucleus.
wrongExplains.3: Multiplication before secretion doesn't define holocrine.

scaffolds.explain: SEBACEOUS (oil) GLANDS use HOLOCRINE secretion. The cells fill with sebum, swell, and eventually rupture — cellular martyrdom for smooth skin.

scaffolds.q: What is the key characteristic that defines holocrine glands?
options: The secreting cells rupture and die to release their product
scaffolds.explain: HOLOCRINE glands are defined by CELL RUPTURE and DEATH during secretion. Unlike merocrine (cell lives) or apocrine (cell loses apex), holocrine means TOTAL cell destruction.

wrongExplains.0: Duct length doesn't define secretion mode.
wrongExplains.2: Volume doesn't define the secretion mechanism.
wrongExplains.3: Secreting into blood describes endocrine glands.

scaffolds.q: Why must holocrine glands constantly replace their cells?
options: The cells die each time they secrete
scaffolds.explain: Holocrine glands must constantly REPLACE CELLS because each cell DIES when it secretes. Stem cells at the base divide to replenish the secreting population.

wrongExplains.0: Cells don't migrate; they rupture in place.
wrongExplains.2: Size isn't the issue; cell death is.
wrongExplains.3: The whole cell is lost, not just the nucleus.

scaffolds.q: Glands whose secretions are released when cells rupture and die are classified as ___?
scaffolds.explain: HOLOCRINE glands release secretions when cells RUPTURE and DIE. The entire cell becomes the secretion — sebaceous glands are the classic example.

wrongExplains.0: Merocrine cells stay intact during secretion.
wrongExplains.2: Apocrine cells only lose their apex, not the whole cell.
wrongExplains.3: Endocrine describes gland type (ductless), not secretion mode.
